metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_153", "source_md": "Long-term observations of oxygenated volatile orga.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Long-term observations of oxygenated volatile orga\\figure_7.jpg", "title": "Fig. 7. Change in ambient concentrations of OVOCs contributed by different sources during the dry seasons of 2014-2019.", "references": ["Fig. 7 presents the changes in absolute concentrations of OVOCs contributed by different sources during the dry seasons for the six-year study period. To better depict the contribution trends of different OVOC sources, the parameters were normalized (based on", "Oxygenated volatile organic compounds (OVOCs) are important precursors and intermediate products of atmospheric photochemical reactions, which can promote the formation of secondary pollutants such as ozone  $(\\mathrm{O}_3)$  and secondary organic aerosol (SOA). However, there have been few studies on the sources of and long-term variation in ambient OVOCs. This study combined sensitive, near real-time measurements of VOCs by proton transfer reaction-mass spectrometry (PTR-MS) with an improved photochemical age parameterization method to quantify daytime sources of OVOCs in an urban atmosphere in China from 2014 to 2019, permitting the observation of the impacts of emission control strategies that were implemented during this period. Temporal variation in six key OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, methyl ethyl ketone (MEK), formic acid, and acetic acid) were observed. The sum of concentrations of OVOCs was averagely  $13\\%$  higher during the dry season (November to April), when winds transported polluted air masses to Shenzhen from the continent, than during the wet season, and peak diurnal levels occurred during the daytime year-round due to photochemical production and higher daytime anthropogenic emissions. The average dry season concentration of OVOCs declined from a peak of 30.3 ppb in 2015 to 18.7 ppb in 2019. The results of source apportionment showed that primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to methanol, MEK, and acetic acid  $(32 - 51\\%)$ ; the dominant sources of acetaldehyde and formic acid were both primary and secondary anthropogenic sources; and biomass burning contributed a small fraction  $(5 - 11\\%)$  to the six OVOCs. From 2014 to 2019, contributions from primary anthropogenic sources of OVOCs decreased significantly by  $50 - 60\\%$  due to intensive pollution control measures in Shenzhen, whereas pollution control measures had no observable impact on secondary OVOCs, indicating their formation was not limited by availability of their primary VOC precursors.", "Previous studies have proven that some low-carbon OVOCs (formaldehyde and acetaldehyde) can generate OH radicals and hydroperoxyl radicals  $\\mathrm{(HO_2)}$  through photolysis to affect the oxidizing capacity of the atmosphere (Volkamer et al., 2010; Shao et al., 2011; Xue et al., 2016). OVOCs can also participate in photochemical reactions and contribute to the generation of  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  and SOA (Louie et al., 2013; Ehn et al., 2014). In addition, as most OVOCs are highly water soluble, they can be absorbed into the human body, irritating the eyes, skin and respiratory tract, thus harming human health. OVOCs such as formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, acetone and acrolein have been shown to pose serious health risks (WHO, 2000, Delfino et al., 2003; Bashkin, 2009; Du et al., 2014). Given the important role of OVOCs in photochemical reactions and the complexity of their sources, OVOCs have been the subject of extensive research recently (Sahu et al., 2017; Maji et al., 2020), especially in China, where air pollution is severe (Zhang et al., 2012, 2015; Zhou et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019). Zhang et al. (2012) studied the pollution characteristics of BTEX (benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, m,p-xylene and o-xylene) and carbonyl compounds during summer in Beijing and found that the  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  formation potential of carbonyl compounds  $(166.1~\\mu \\mathrm{g} / \\mathrm{m}^3)$  was approximately 2.5 times that of BTEX  $(65.4~\\mu \\mathrm{g} / \\mathrm{m}^3)$ . Zhou et al. (2019) used proton transfer reaction mass spectrometry (PTR-MS) to measure VOCs in Xi'an and used the positive matrix factorization (PMF) model to analyze over 30 types of VOCs and identify eight primary sources and found that local emissions, such as industrial processes, solvent usage, residential biofuel use and vehicular exhaust, contributed about  $70\\%$  of the VOCs measured. Zhu et al. (2019) used PTR-MS to monitor OVOCs during different seasons and applied photochemical age parameter analysis to evaluate the sources of OVOCs in Shenzhen. They found that primary anthropogenic sources were the most important source of OVOCs in urban areas of China.", "Shenzhen belongs to the subtropical marine monsoon climate zone. Marked differences in the four seasons were not evident, and the observation time each year was slightly different; therefore, this study divided the entire year into a dry season and a wet season according to the monthly temperature and precipitation. As presented in Figure S1, the period from May to October, with higher temperatures and abundant precipitation, was defined as the wet season, and the period from November to April, with less precipitation, was defined as the dry season. The meteorological data are from the Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality (Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). In addition to the differences in temperature and precipitation, the sources of air masses in Shenzhen during the wet and dry seasons were also very different. Taking January and July 2018 as an example, the 48-h backward trajectories at  $100\\mathrm{m}$  above ground level in Shenzhen  $(113.9^{\\circ}\\mathrm{E}, 22.6^{\\circ}\\mathrm{N})$  were calculated beginning at 12:00 UTC using the Hybrid Single Particle Lagrangian Integrated Trajectory (HYSPLIT) model (Draxler and Rolph, 2003). The results show that the origin of the air mass passing through Shenzhen in different seasons was very different: during the dry season, the air mass mainly came from the northern mainland, and during the wet season, it mainly came from the South China Sea, which has also been explained in detail in our previous studies (Zou et al., 2017). In addition, referencing existing studies of air pollution in the Pearl River Delta, many scholars have adopted a method of dividing the wet and dry seasons (Cui et al., 2015). Figure S2 shows the changes in major meteorological parameters during the six-year observation period in Shenzhen. Analysis of major meteorological parameters in Shenzhen (Fig S2) did not indicate significant interannual variability in temperature, rainfall, humidity or wind conditions that could have substantially influenced the long-term variation in atmospheric pollutants observed in this study.", "PRD and to improve the accuracy of the source apportionment. First, we chose benzene as the tracer of primary anthropogenic sources of OVOCs. Generally, CO (de Gouw et al., 2005, 2018), acetylene (Yuan et al., 2012) and benzene (Ma et al., 2016; Zhu et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2019) are commonly used tracers representing anthropogenic sources of VOCs. Anthropogenic emissions of VOCs in China are rather complicated, with industrial solvents and motor vehicle exhaust contributing significantly to VOCs. However, CO and acetylene mainly come from combustion emissions, which cannot represent well the anthropogenic emissions of VOCs in China. Compared with CO and acetylene, benzene can better reflect the anthropogenic emissions of VOCs in the PRD. For example, Zhang et al. (2013) used the PMF model to analyze the source of VOCs in the PRD and found that combustion processes and industrial emissions were the predominant sources of benzene. Second, considering the photolysis of aldehydes and ketones during the daytime, which obviously conflicts with assumption (2), we need to consider the effect of the photodegradation of aldehydes and ketones on their removal. Referring to the studies of Wang et al. (2017) and Huang et al. (2020), we introduced a coefficient  $(\\mathrm{m})$  before the OH reaction rate constant  $\\left(\\mathrm{k}_{\\mathrm{ovoc}}\\right)$  to compensate for the daytime loss of aldehydes and ketones caused by photolysis. A detailed description of the calculation method of the m value is given in the supporting information. For methanol, formic acid and acetic acid, with almost no photolysis (Saunders et al., 2003), this study ignored the effect of photolysis on daytime removal (set m as 1), while the calculated m values for acetaldehyde, acetone and methyl ethyl ketone (MEK) were 1.02, 1.26 and 1.25, respectively, during the sampling period. Third, previous studies have shown that biomass burning emissions are an inevitable source of OVOCs (Yuan et al., 2010; Warneke et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2016). For example, Yuan et al. (2010) reported that biomass burning emissions in the PRD contributed  $9.5 - 17.7\\%$  to nine kinds of VOCs (including methanol, acetone, and MEK), suggesting that the contribution of biomass burning to OVOCs cannot be ignored. Therefore, the last parameter  $(ER_{bb} * [\\text{Tracer}_{bb}])$  in Equation (1) was added in this study to estimate the contribution of biomass burning to OVOCs.  $[\\text{Tracer}_{bb}]$  represents the concentration of ambient acetonitrile, which is an excellent indicator for biomass burning and can be accurately measured by PTR-MS (de Gouw et al., 2003b). In addition, previous studies have shown that benzene mainly comes from transportation and solvent use in the PRD, and biomass burning accounts for less than  $10\\%$  (Lyu et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2013), so the influence of using benzene as a tracer of anthropogenic sources on biomass burning emissions can be ignored.  $\\mathrm{ER}_{bb}$  is the emission factor of OVOCs relative to acetonitrile, which was calculated by least-squares fit along with  $\\mathrm{ER}_{\\mathrm{OVOC}}$ ,  $\\mathrm{ER}_{\\mathrm{precursor}}$ ,  $\\mathrm{ER}_{\\mathrm{biogen}}$ ,  $\\mathrm{k}_{\\mathrm{precursor}}$ , and [background].", "It should be emphasized that because the sampling data of this study span six years, in addition to the above assumptions, one more assumption should be added: (4) during the sampling period, there was no significant change in the ratio of OVOC anthropogenic emissions to tracers (benzene) in Shenzhen. The emission reduction strategy of Shenzhen is to reduce emission sources of pollutants as a whole, such as replacing gasoline vehicles with electric vehicles and closing down or moving VOC-emitting enterprises to other areas. These measures have reduced emissions of VOCs, but the emission ratio among specific VOC species is relatively stable. Toluene/benzene (T/B) was considered a good indicator to judge the contribution of motor vehicle sources and solvent use to VOC emissions (Nelson and Quigley, 1984; Wang et al., 2002; Araizaga et al., 2013). Figure S5 shows the changes in the concentrations of benzene and toluene and T/B from 2014 to 2019. The concentrations of benzene and toluene in the six years showed a downward trend, while T/B remained at approximately 5, which also indicated", "A total of 17 species of VOCs were monitored in this study by PTR-MS from 2014 to 2019, including 6 species classified as OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, formic acid, acetic acid, and MEK); 5 aromatic hydrocarbons (benzene, toluene, styrene, C8 and C9 aromatics); 3 classified as biogenic species (isoprene, MVK + MACR, and monoterpenes); and 2 species that, in addition to benzene, were measured as tracers (acetonitrile and DMS). The compounds, the respective  $\\mathrm{m} / \\mathrm{z}$  of their parent ion signal, and their detection limits are provided in Table S3. As shown in Fig. 2, the average of the sum of the VOC concentrations in the dry season over the six years ranged from 25.3 to 41.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 33.1 ppbv; for the wet season, the average value ranged from 23.0 to 39.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 29.4 ppbv. In terms of the proportion of each component, in the six years of dry and wet seasons, OVOCs (70–78%) accounted for the largest proportion, followed by aromatic hydrocarbons (16–26%), whereas the concentrations of biogenic VOCs (BVOCs) and tracers were relatively low (less than 10%). Figure S6 shows the time series of 17 species of VOCs during the sampling period. To evaluate the concentrations of OVOCs in this study, comparative results of the concentrations of six OVOCs in five urban areas in China are listed in Table 1. Compared to Guangzhou and Hong Kong, two other mega-cities in the PRD region, the concentrations of acetaldehyde, acetone, and MEK in Shenzhen were higher than those in Hong", "Fig. 2. Concentrations of VOC components during dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019.", "The parameterization method based on photochemical aging described in section 2.4 was used to analyze the sources of OVOCs during the daytime (8:00-20:00). Referencing the source analysis results for the OVOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019 (in Table 2), the measured concentrations of six OVOC species monitored by PTR-MS were in good agreement with the simulated concentrations, and the measured values of each OVOC species were highly correlated with the simulated values (R was between 0.71 and 0.86, except for formic acid, which was 0.62). For the six-year average, the sources of methanol and MEK were similar, with the largest contribution from primary anthropogenic sources  $(47 - 51\\%)$  followed by biogenic and background sources. The contributions to acetaldehyde and acetic acid from primary and secondary anthropogenic sources were similar  $(26 - 32\\%)$  followed by biogenic sources (approximately  $20\\%$ ). In contrast to other OVOCs, acetone was the most affected by regional background concentration  $(37\\%)$  which may be because acetone is relatively inactive and widely exists in the atmosphere, and its ground-level background mixing ratios can reach 500 pptv throughout the troposphere under extremely clean conditions (Seinfeld and Pandis, 2016). For formic acid, secondary anthropogenic sources contributed the most  $(34\\%)$", "Fig. 6 shows the trends in the relative contributions to OVOCs from 2014 to 2019 during the dry and wet seasons. In general, during the six years from 2014 to 2019, primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to OVOCs during Shenzhen's dry season, but the proportion exhibited a decreasing trend year over year, and the proportion during the six-year period decreased by approximately  $15\\%$  overall. This continuous decreasing trend indicates that the VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas, where most of the industry and traffic of Shenzhen is located, had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. The OVOC source compositions during the wet season, when clean marine air masses from the south dominated the sampling site, did not exhibit a clear year-by-year trend. The contributions of secondary anthropogenic sources to OVOC concentrations (except that of methanol) during the wet season were significantly higher (approximately 1.3-1.6 times greater) than those during the dry season, indicating that secondary transformation of NMHCs during the wet season contributed greatly to OVOC concentrations. Biogenic sources reasonably contributed a larger proportion of OVOC concentrations during the wet season, whereas biomass burning contributed a larger fraction of acetaldehyde during the dry season.", "the OVOC concentrations in 2014). From 2014 to 2019, the ambient concentration of OVOCs contributed by primary anthropogenic sources decreased significantly, declining by  $50 - 60\\%$ . Among the six OVOC species, the species with the largest decrease in concentration attributed to primary anthropogenic sources was methanol, which decreased by 7 ppb. However, contributions of the other sources to the absolute concentrations of OVOCs did not significantly change over the six-year period. Specifically, annual variations in secondary anthropogenic sources were not significant, indicating that secondary formation of atmospheric OVOCs in this region might be restricted by the atmospheric oxidizing capacity and that precursor emissions are more than abundant.", "hydrocarbon and 490 tons of NOx. VOC emission reduction in industrial enterprises and the coating industry occurs mainly through upgrading emission standards and closing down industries with high VOC emissions. For example, furniture manufacturing used to be an important industry in Shenzhen but also a key VOC-emitting industry. From 2010 to 2016, 565 coating production lines were removed or relocated in Shenzhen, reducing VOC emissions by more than 30,000 tons every year. Since 2017, Shenzhen has completely banned solvent paints and adhesives in the manufacturing process and replaced them with water-based paints to further reduce VOC emissions, especially the emissions of aldehydes and ketones commonly used in the production of furniture manufacturing (Ecological Environment Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). As a commonly used tracer of anthropogenic sources of VOCs, the annual average concentration of benzene during the dry season from 2014 to 2019 was 0.98, 0.81, 0.64, 0.61, 0.49, and 0.45 ppb (Figure S5), respectively, reflecting the decreasing trend in primary VOC emissions in recent years. Therefore, the decrease in OVOC concentrations during the dry season over the six-year study period could primarily be attributed to decreases in the contributions from primary anthropogenic sources. However, the contribution of secondary anthropogenic", "PTR-MS was used for continuous online monitoring of atmospheric VOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019. The results revealed that OVOCs were the most abundant of 17 species of VOCs measured, comprising  $70 - 78\\%$ . The six-year mean concentration for the dry season was  $33.1~\\mathrm{ppb}$ , approximately  $13\\%$  higher than that for the wet season of  $22.9~\\mathrm{ppb}$ . There were diurnal variations in OVOC concentrations, with higher concentrations during the daytime. The diurnal variation in acetaldehyde was most similar to that of  $\\mathrm{O}_3$ , and it had the largest OFP of the OVOC species, indicating that acetaldehyde was an important intermediate of photochemical reactions. The results from source apportionment based on photochemical aging parameterization revealed that contributions of OVOCs from primary anthropogenic sources exhibited an evident declining trend of  $50 - 60\\%$  from 2014 to 2019 during the dry season, indicating that the intensive VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas of the sampling site had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. However, to reduce the impact of OVOCs on air quality in Shenzhen, more efforts are needed to control OVOCs from anthropogenic secondary sources by reducing not only their primary VOC precursors but also their complex atmospheric oxidizing capacity, which will finally restrict the production of  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  and SOA.", "sources are responsible for the volatile organic compounds in the atmosphere of Pearl River Delta. img_153.source_reason: 考虑到政策干预对PM2.5的显著改善，探索生态文明对空气质量的影响有助于理解其长期效果和协调不同生态措施的综合作用。

evaluation.extracted_insight: 图表展示 OVOCs 在 2014-2019 年干季期间，由于严格的污染控制措施导致的主要人为源贡献显著减少，从而整体环境浓度下降。
evaluation.reason: 证据与干季污染物来源变化的长期趋势分析相关，且图示来源明确，有助于理解污染控制的成效。

content.title: Figure 5. Emissions distributions by Asian regions in 2010.
content.summary: The figure shows 2010 emissions distribution of various species across Asian regions, highlighting China's dominance in most emissions categories and India's significant contribution.
content.insight: Figure 5 illustrates that China and India are the leading contributors to emissions in Asia, with China contributing over 50% to several species, demonstrating regional emission disparities and source contributions.

China Other East Asia India Other South Asia Southeast Asia Central Asia Russia Asia", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "$31.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ ,  $22.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ ,  $3.5\mathrm{TgBC}$ ,  $8.3\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Emissions from China and India dominate the emissions of Asia for most of the species. We also estimated Asian emissions in 2006 using the same methodology of MIX. The relative change rates of Asian emissions for the period of 2006-2010 are estimated as follows:  $-8.1\%$  for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $+19.2\%$  for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ ,  $+3.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}$ ,  $+15.5\%$  for NMVOC,  $+1.7\%$  for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $-3.4\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $-1.6\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $+5.5\%$  for BC,  $+1.8\%$  for OC, and  $+19.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Model-ready speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were developed following a profile-assignment approach. Monthly gridded emissions at a spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  are developed and can be accessed from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix.", "Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).", "Recent studies (e.g., Zhang et al., 2009; Kurokawa et al., 2013) tend to use the mosaic approach to supplement the Asian emission inventory developments. To support the NASA's INTEX-B (the Intercontinental Chemical Transport Experiment Phase B) mission (van Donkelaar et al., 2008; Adhikary et al., 2010), Zhang et al. (2009) developed a new emission inventory for Asia for the year 2006 as an update and improvement of the TRACE-P inventory (Streets et al., 2003). Compared to the TRACE-P inventory, the INTEX-B inventory improved emission estimates for China by introducing a technology-based methodology and incorporated several local inventories including BC and OC emissions for India from Reddy et al. (2002a, b), a Japan emission inventory from Kannari et al. (2007), and official emission inventories for the Republic of Korea and Taiwan. In the updated version 2.1 of the REAS inventory (Kurokawa et al., 2013), a few regional inventories developed with local knowledge are also incorporated to improve the accuracy (see Sect. 2.2.1 for details).", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in", "We used anthropogenic emissions from REAS2 (Kurokawa et al., 2013) to fill the gap where local emission data are not available. REAS2 updated the REAS version 1.1 for both activity data and emission factors by each country and region using global and regional statistics and recent regional specific studies on emissions factors. REAS2 extended the domain to include emissions of Central Asia and the Asian part of Russia (referred to as Russia Asia). Readers can refer to Kurokawa et al. SO2 CO PM25 CO2", "Monthly emissions by sector and by Asian region are provided in Tables S6-S14. Monthly profiles in emissions are highly sector dependent given that monthly activity rates vary among different sectors. Figure 10 illustrates the monthly variations of Asian  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , CO,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions by sector for the year 2010. Different species generally show similar monthly emission patterns within the same sector, indicating that monthly emission profiles of each sector are dominated by monthly variations in activity rates. For example, industrial emissions are higher in the second half of the year induced by larger industrial productions to meet the annual total production target. The most significant monthly variation with a winter peak was found in the residential sector, reflecting the higher energy demand for residential heating in winter. Residential  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions in winter are even higher than other species, because  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions from China dominate residential emissions in Asia (70% of total), of which coal consumption in winter is higher than other regions for heating. Monthly profiles of CO emissions are different from other species for the transportation sector. This is because the CO emission factor in winter is higher than in other seasons due to additional emissions from the cold-start process (Kurokawa et al., 2013; Zheng et al., 2014).", "In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. The differences between REAS and MIX over China and India will be discussed in the following sections.", "though a point-by-point comparison of emission factors between EDGAR v4.2 and MIX is not feasible, we can still speculate that EDGAR v4.2 may overestimate the combustion efficiency and emission control measures in Asia by using an emission factor database from developed countries.  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  emissions in EDGAR v4.2 are  $26\%$  lower than in MIX, with a large difference in residential emissions. The differences are mainly from high emission estimates of wastewater treatment sources in REAS2, which were incorporated into MIX for Asian regions except China. MIX estimated  $3.4\mathrm{Tg}\mathrm{NH}_3$  emissions from wastewater treatment in Asia in 2008, which are more than 2 orders of magnitude higher than EDGAR v4.2 estimates. Differences in  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions at the sector level are also large; similar estimates of  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions in the two datasets are rather a coincidence than real agreements.", "The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).", "Table 9. Uncertainty in emission estimates by Asian regions in 2010 (95 % confidence intervals; unit: %).", "In this work, we developed a new anthropogenic emission inventory for Asia for the years of 2008 and 2010 by constructing a mosaic of several regional and national emission inventories. MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. Gridded emissions are available from the following website: http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix.", "Adhikary, B., Carmichael, G. content.title: Fig. 1. Comprehensive traffic monitoring network in Xiaoshan District. (a) Xiaoshan District (the red area) is located in the hinterland of the YRD in China. (b) Comprehensive traffic monitoring achieves full coverage over Xiaoshan District. Each dot represents a set of comprehensive traffic monitoring facilities that can recognize license plates, traffic fluxes, vehicle-specific speed, and vehicle categories. The entire road network over Xiaoshan District is divided into 4585 road segments. Such road segments are divided into three road classes: highways (blue lines), arterial roads (pink lines), and residential streets (green lines). Map data © 2022, Gaode Map.
content.summary: The figure illustrates the comprehensive traffic monitoring network in Xiaoshan District, highlighting road classes and monitoring facilities on a map.
content.insight: The traffic monitoring network in Xiaoshan enables detailed tracking of vehicle dynamics, crucial for analyzing the impact of traffic on air quality and public health.

content.title: Fig. 2. Comparisons of emission uncertainties of coal-fired power plants in Guizhou in 2005 using sector-based and unit-based methods. The “+” represents the central value and the box represents the  95\%  CI using the unit-based method, while the larger range line represents the  95\%  CI using the sector-based method.
content.summary: Figure 2 compares emissions uncertainties of coal-fired power plants in Guizhou, 2005, using sector-based and unit-based methods. Central values and 95% CIs are shown for each pollutant.
content.insight: The figure demonstrates that using a unit-based method reduces emission uncertainty compared to the sector-based approach, highlighting the importance of detailed source information.

As shown in Fig. 2, the  $95\\%$  CIs using the sector-based method is largest for  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ $(-30\\% \\sim 108\\%)$  and smallest for  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ $(-25\\% \\sim 20\\%)$ . Compared to  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , the  $95\\%$  CI of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  emission is relatively large, reaching  $-40\\% \\sim 42\\%$ , and the variation of sulfur contents is estimated to contribute almost  $90\\%$  to that uncertainty. Employing a unit-based method, the  $95\\%$  CIs for all species are significantly reduced, with the largest ranging  $-9\\% \\sim 18\\%$ , for  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and the smallest  $-8\\% \\sim 8\\%$ , for  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ . Figure 3 shows the difference in distributions of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions applying the two methods. content.title: Fig. 2. Emission factors of  PM_2.5 emitted from 10 tested vehicles.
content.summary: The figure displays PM2.5 emission factors for gasoline and diesel vehicles under different China emission standards (III, IV, V).
content.insight: As emission standards improve, PM2.5 emission factors decrease. Diesel vehicles emit higher PM2.5 levels than gasoline vehicles under the same standard.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_170", "source_md": "On-road emissions of fine particles and associated.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\On-road emissions of fine particles and associated\\figure_2.jpg", "title": "Fig. 2. Emission factors of  PM_2.5 emitted from 10 tested vehicles.", "references": ["The  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions of six gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 2. There was only one China III gasoline vehicle and its  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor was  $1.675\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Three gasoline vehicles with China IV emission standard had an average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of  $1.452\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . The average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of other two China V gasoline vehicles was  $0.783\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . The  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of gasoline vehicles decreased gradually with the increasing emission standards. Four diesel vehicles were tested in this experiment as well. Only one China III diesel vehicle was tested and its  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor was  $33.095\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Two diesel vehicles with China IV emission standard had an average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of  $15.134\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . There was only one China V diesel vehicle and its  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor was  $2.994\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . As the emission standards rose up from China III to China V, the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of diesel vehicles decreased by more than  $90\\%$ . As emission standards became stricter, the emission factor of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  gradually decreased because vehicles with higher emission standards usually had better exhaust control devices. Meanwhile, vehicles with higher emission standards were often younger than vehicles with lower emission standards (Zhang et al., 2019a, b). Under the same emission standards, the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of diesel vehicles were obviously higher than gasoline vehicles, because the molecules that made up diesel were usually larger than gasoline (Distillation range of gasoline and diesel see Table s5.), and the engines could not be evenly mixed with air during", "Vehicle emission is an important contributor to urban air pollution with the increasing number of motor vehicles. Ten typical vehicles were selected in Wuhan to study the emissions of fine particular matters  $\\mathrm{(PM_{2.5})}$  and associated chemical components by on-road tests through a Portable Emission Monitoring System (PEMS). The emission factors of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and the compositions of it from different types of vehicle were obtained. Results showed that the average emission factors of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline and diesel vehicles were 1.266 and  $16.589\\mathrm{mg}/$  km. As the emission standard of vehicles increased from China III to China V,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor gradually decreased from 17.385 to  $1.520~\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Emission rate of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was  $0.0384~\\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed, and it increased to 0.0775 and  $0.0964~\\mathrm{mg / s}$  under the medium and high speeds. The ratio of organic carbon versus elemental carbon (OC/EC) in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles was 6.89, which was greater than that of diesel vehicles as 3.12. Because gasoline was made of small molecules and the compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder. The top four water-soluble ions with high emission factors were  $\\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_4^{2 - }$ ,  $\\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + }$ , and  $\\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , while K, Na, Ca and Mg had a larger emission factors in the 21 tested inorganic elements. These water-soluble ions and inorganic elements mainly came from the oil burning, fuel additives and engines wear. Results of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission characteristics would help to improve the air quality in Wuhan.", "As the pollution of vehicle emission becomes a prominent social problem, the monitoring methods of vehicle emissions are developed and improved continuously (Giechaskiel et al., 2014; Xue et al., 2018; Farwick et al., 2019). These methods mainly include remote sensing detection, vehicle bench test, tunnel test and portable vehicle emission measurement (Giechaskiel et al., 2018; Mahesh et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2020a, b). Remote sensing is an effective and economic tool to monitor vehicle emissions, while it is accurate in identifying gasoline vehicles but not diesel ones (Huang et al., 2018). And the chemical composition of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  can not be obtained by remote sensing (Smit et al., 2019). Myung et al. (2009) carried out bench tests on light motor vehicles and found that  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions of using liquefied petroleum gas and biodiesel were much lower than those of gasoline and diesel. Carbonaceous components in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from vehicles could be studied through a bench test, and it found that the driving speed directly affected the EC emissions (Fraser et al., 2002). However, the fluence of actual driving conditions on vehicle emissions could not be reflected (Pouresmaeili et al., 2018). Sánchez et al. (2019) studied the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of both light and heavy vehicles through tunnel experiments, and the emission factors of heavy vehicles tended to be much higher than those of light vehicles. Tunnel experiments were also conducted by Hao et al. (2019) to study their characteristics of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from motor vehicles in Xi'an, China, and the results showed that OC accounted for  $34.10\\%$  of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . Since tunnels in different locations have different speed limits, and it is difficult to cover both high-speed and real-world driving conditions in some tunnels (Huang et al., 2017; Smit et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2020a, b). Huang et al. (2013) used PEMS to detect the concentrations of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from buses, and cars under actual driving conditions, and they found that concentrations of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  increased with the driving speed. PEMS was also used to analyze the chemical composition of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from heavy diesel trucks, and it was found that EC and OC were the major components of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (Zhang et al., 2015). Based on the results of previous researches, the conclusions drawn from various cities were obviously different due to their geographical locations, climates, populations, etc. Wuhan is located in the central of China, and it has a large population and a large number of motor vehicles with high humidity climate (Yang et al., 2019). The effect of humidity on some gaseous pollutants (such as CO and NOx) emitted from vehicles has been studied (Dolly et al., 2020), while few research were carried out on the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and its chemical components. Therefore, it was necessary to carry out the research on vehicle emissions of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in Wuhan, China.", "from vehicles became outstanding in Wuhan. In order to find out the characteristics of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and associated chemical components from on-road emissions in urban areas in Wuhan, which was located in the central and southern China and the climates were high temperature and humidity, ten typical motor vehicles were selected to carry out this research.  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors and emission concentration characteristics of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from motor vehicles under different emission standards at different operating speeds were performed. The characteristics of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  components, including carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements, were detected as well.  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  composition spectrum of vehicle emission in Wuhan would be finally obtained. Results of this study would help formulating measures for the reduction of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from vehicles in Wuhan, and provide a reference for  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution control of motor vehicles in similar types.", "$\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles were collected by the SEMTECH-PFS sub-module. SEMTECH-PFS sub-module had three channels for membrane sampling, where three sampling membranes with a diameter of  $47\\mathrm{mm}$  were located. These three channels could not be used to collect  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  at the same time. When one channel was ventilated and sampling, the other two channels were closed. Computer was used to decide the channel of the airflow. Before sampling started, a quartz filter (MK360, Munktel, Sweden) was placed on Channel 1, a Teflon filter (R2PJ047, PALL, USA) was placed on Channel 2 and another quartz filter was placed on Chanel 3. The quartz filter membrane on Chanel 1 was mainly used for analyzing organic carbon, elemental carbon and water-soluble ions. The Teflon filter membrane on Chanel 2 was used for analyzing elements. The quartz filter membrane on Chanel 3 was used as the blank sample.", "Carbonaceous aerosols consisted of organic carbon (OC), elemental carbon (EC), and a small amount of carbonate. Since carbonates were very small part in atmospheric particles and the proportion of carbonates was less than  $5\\%$  of total carbon (TC), they would not be discussed in this study. The DRI thermal carbon analyzer (IMPROVE-A, DRI, USA) was used to detect the concentration of OC and EC, when  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  sample was collected without the pretreatment after equilibrium weighing. Several water-soluble ions in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles were analyzed, which included  $\\mathrm{K}^{+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{NH}_4^+$ ,  $\\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_3^-$  and  $\\mathrm{F}^-$ . Ion chromatograph (IC) was used to analyze the water-soluble ions that collected on the filter. 21 crustal elements including Na, Mg, K, Ca, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Ga, As, Rb, Sr, Ag, Cd, Cs, Ba and Pb were detected by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). Microwave digester was used as the pretreatment of collected filter samples. Combining the exhaust gas flow data recorded by the exhaust gas flow meter and the vehicle driving conditions data recorded by the GPS, the emission rate under the actual driving conditions could be calculated. According to the driving speed, the driving conditions of the tested vehicles were divided into eight speed segments: idle speed (0 km/h), 0–10 km/h, 0–20 km/h, 20–30 km/h, 30–40 km/h, 40–50 km/h, 50–60 km/h and >60 km/h. The average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates of the tested vehicles in all the eight speed segments were calculated, and the results were shown in Fig. 3.", "Table 2 Emission factors of carbonaceous components in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from vehicles.", "In Table 2, the average emission factors of TC for China III, IV and V gasoline vehicles were 1086.15, 1054.06 and  $397.94~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The OC/EC ratios of carbonaceous compositions from the 10 tested vehicles were shown in Fig. 4, and the average OC/EC ratios of the vehicles with different emission standards were figured out as well. The OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was higher than 3.0 except for Vehicle 1#. It meant there were many organic components in the carbonaceous component, which was consistent with the results of some recent studies (James et al., 2002). The OC/EC ratio of Vehicle 1# was only 0.3, which deviated from the normal value of gasoline vehicle emissions. Because Vehicle 1# was a taxi with both gasoline and natural gas functions, and the engine used a mixture of gasoline and natural gas for a long time. It used gasoline fuel during the test, and insufficient combustion of gasoline in the engine caused an increase in the number of black carbon particles, which led to a small ratio of OC/EC. The average OC/EC ratio of China IV Vehicles was 10.2, and it decreased to 5.3 in China V gasoline vehicles. This indicated the improvement of emission standards could reduce the percentage of OC in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from gasoline vehicles. For diesel vehicles, the average OC/EC ratios of China III, IV and V vehicles were 1.5, 3.0, and 4.9, respectively. The OC/EC ratio of diesel vehicles turned to decrease as the emission standard rose up. It could also be seen from Fig. 4 that the OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was generally greater than that of diesel vehicles, which was mainly because diesel was more likely to form unburned soot particles during the combustion process (Shen et al., 2021). There were two main differences between gasoline vehicles and diesel vehicles, fuel composition and engine structure. Gasoline was made of small molecules and was easy to mix evenly with air. After the spark plug discharged ignition, the fuel burned rapidly. content.title: Fig. 7. a, mean diurnal cycle of simulated  PM_{2.5}  concentrations, downward surface shortwave radiation based on case 5, as well as their changes due to aerosol radiative effects ((case 5 - case 6) - (case 1 - case 2)) over Heilongjiang (top black box in Fig. S1b) from November 1 to 3, 2017. b, the same as a, but the meteorological parameters were changed to PBL height.
content.summary: The figure shows diurnal cycles of PM2.5 concentrations and atmospheric parameters over Heilongjiang, illustrating changes due to aerosol radiative effects over a three-day period in November 2017.
content.insight: The analysis demonstrates how aerosol radiative effects influence PM2.5 concentrations and meteorological parameters, providing insights into air quality dynamics in Heilongjiang.
